nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas of lagoons and seaward reefs, also found around sheltered rocky reef habitats.

decorPreferences: deep fine-sand bed for burrowing and foraging
abundant mature live rock to host copepod populations
attached or plumbed refugium recommended as ongoing pod source

diet.notes: The Scooter Blenny is a specialist micro-predator that grazes almost continuously on tiny benthic copepods — particularly bottom-dwelling species such as Tisbe and Tigriopus — along with amphipods and other small invertebrates that live between sand grains. Many specimens slowly starve in undersized or young systems because they rarely accept prepared foods readily. A mature reef (ideally 6+ months) with an established plumbed refugium producing a continuous copepod supply is generally considered essential. Some individuals can be coaxed onto live or vitamin-enriched frozen brine, mysis, or fish eggs via target-feeding with a turkey baster, but this should be treated as supplementation, not as a replacement for live pods.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish that do not compete for pods
avoidWith: Mandarin dragonets and other Synchiropus species in the same tank (compete for the same pod food source)
boisterous or fast-feeding tankmates such as wrasses, anthias, damsels, and chromis that outcompete dragonets at feeding time
rival conspecific males in tanks under ~200 L
predatory fish that may target a slow-moving bottom dweller

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Jeffery Beggerly; 640x448; sharp aquarium male with full ornate dorsal sail displayed showing diagnostic blue ocelli, green maze pattern and orange striping over mottled body; whole fish, vivid.
Brian Jeffery Beggerly; 640x427; sharp side portrait of a different individual showing blue face spotting, maroon/cream mottling and red body bars; adds angle/individual variety.
Izuzuki; 640x480; sharp in-situ reef shot of a male displaying the ocellated dorsal sail; vibrant, whole fish, natural-habitat variety.

summary: The Scooter Blenny is a small Indo-Pacific dragonet — not a true blenny — prized for its mottled markings and hover-and-perch behavior, but notoriously difficult to keep because it is a specialist copepod feeder that tends to starve in immature or undersized reef systems.
careNotes: Despite the trade name, this species is not a blenny — it is a Scooter Dragonet in family Callionymidae, the same family as the Mandarinfish, and it shares the Mandarinfish's notorious feeding caveat. The defining husbandry challenge is diet: scooter dragonets graze almost continuously on tiny benthic copepods and amphipods, and a typical young or small reef cannot produce pods fast enough to keep one alive. A mature reef tank with an established plumbed refugium (chaeto or similar macro, sand/rubble, and a dedicated pod-culture inflow) is generally considered the minimum viable setup; many specimens introduced to systems without this support slowly waste away over weeks to months. Some individuals can be transitioned to vitamin-enriched live or frozen foods (baby brine, Tisbe/Tigriopus pods, mysis, fish eggs) via gentle target-feeding, but this should supplement, not replace, an established live pod population. They are reef-safe and ignore corals and ornamental invertebrates. Conspecifics are best kept as a single male with one or two females introduced simultaneously; multiple males will harass each other in anything smaller than a long footprint. Tankmate choice is critical — avoid wrasses, anthias, damsels, and chromis that strip pods from the rockwork before the dragonet can find them. As scaleless dragonets, they are sensitive to copper-based medications, so quarantine treatment should rely on hyposalinity or copper-free protocols.
breedingNotes: Pelagic spawners observed in captivity but rarely reared. Males display elevated dorsal fins and perform a vertical ascent with the female to release gametes into the water column. Larvae are tiny and require successive sizes of cultured microplankton; commercial captive-breeding has not become routine the way it has for clownfish or some other dragonets.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Provided baseline care parameters: 30 gal minimum, 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, peaceful, reef-safe, difficult care level, carnivore. Originally-supplied product URL (/product/2467/scooter-blenny) 404'd; navigated to the live product page at /products/scooter-blenny.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Callionymidae, Callionymiformes), max length 8.9 cm TL, depth 1-30 m, distribution Indonesia to Pitcairn / north to Japan / south to Australia, wild diet of small benthic invertebrates, preferred temperature 25.5-29 C (wild).

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Cross-checked feeding burden, sand-bed preference, and tank size. Recommends a 50 gal refugium for pod culture, 75-80 F, pH 7.7-8.3, captive lifespan ~2 years, confirms dragonet (Callionymidae) misidentification as a blenny.

additional.name: Reefapp.net Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-referenced for synonymy (Neosynchiropus ocellatus) and reef-safe / peaceful designation.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawning observed; larvae require sequential live microplankton cultures and are rarely reared to settlement in home systems.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Specialist micro-predator on live copepods; requires a mature reef (typically 6+ months) with an established refugium producing a continuous pod supply. Many specimens slowly starve in undersized or young systems, and prepared foods rarely sustain them long-term.
